content.text: Pact is a consumer-driven contract testing framework. The

of a service — the code that calls it — writes tests that capture exactly what it needs from the provider: which request it will send and what shape of response it requires. Those expectations are serialised to a
— the service being called — then replays each interaction in that file against its own running code and verifies that its real responses satisfy every expectation the consumer declared.
In a GenAI microservice architecture the same pattern applies but the services are LLM-facing. A typical setup has an orchestrator service (consumer) that calls an

(providers). The orchestrator's Pact tests document exactly what JSON shape it expects from each downstream service — which fields it reads, which status codes it handles, what the content-type must be — without making real LLM calls. The tool services verify those contracts independently, catching breaking changes before any deployment.
Pact is not an end-to-end test and not a schema validator. It is a
between teams: the consumer unilaterally declares its needs, the provider independently verifies it can meet them, and a shared

records the outcome. The two sides never need to run simultaneously, which makes this pattern especially effective for organisations where the LLM gateway team and the orchestration team work independently.

GenAI systems are built from composable services: an orchestrator calls an LLM proxy, a retrieval service, a vector store, a guardrails service, and one or more tool APIs. Each of those boundaries can drift independently — a new model deployment changes the response envelope, a tool team adds a required field, a guardrails upgrade changes an error code. Traditional integration testing catches these breaks only when all services run simultaneously, which is slow, expensive, and fragile. Contract testing catches them
, with no live dependencies, as soon as either side changes.

The LLM-specific challenge makes this worse than ordinary microservices. An orchestrator that calls a real LLM to run its integration tests adds seconds of latency, consumes tokens, and receives non-deterministic output — making assertions unreliable. Pact removes the live model entirely from the integration concern: the consumer verifies that the
is correct using a mock, and the provider verifies that its
is correct against the contract. Neither side touches a running model during contract testing.
The decisive advantage for teams shipping fast is the

query. Before promoting any service to production, the CI pipeline asks the Pact Broker: does the version I am about to deploy have a verified contract with every other service that is currently deployed? If not, the deploy is blocked. This turns contract testing into a deployment safety net that works across autonomous teams without requiring a shared staging environment.
Consumer tests run against a Pact mock server; provider tests replay recorded interactions. No LLM API keys are required, no models are invoked, and no quotas are consumed.
Early detection of breaking API changes.
If a tool team renames a response field, the provider verification step fails in their CI before the change is merged, not after the orchestrator breaks in production.
The consumer team documents exactly what it uses from each provider. Provider teams can see which fields are actually consumed and safely remove everything else without guessing.

Because the Broker records verification results per version, the orchestrator team can deploy a new consumer version and immediately know which provider versions are compatible, without coordinating a joint release.
A full Pact test suite for a GenAI orchestrator with five downstream services typically completes in under 30 seconds, compared to minutes for a live integration test suite.
The Pact Broker's web UI shows which consumer-provider pairs are currently compatible, which contracts are pending verification, and the full interaction history for every service pair.
structure and semantics of the API contract

, not the business logic or model quality. A provider can satisfy every Pact interaction perfectly and still return semantically wrong answers from its LLM. Contract testing and evaluation/evals testing are complementary, not substitutes.
Scope is the wire boundary only.
Pact cannot verify that a summarisation service produces accurate summaries, only that it returns a

For stateful providers (those that read from a database or cache), you must write provider state handlers that set up the right state before each interaction is replayed. This adds implementation work.
Pact is designed for synchronous request–response. For async tool invocations over queues, use message Pact or an alternative approach.
matchers loosely, the contract becomes so permissive it fails to catch real breaks. Over-matching defeats the purpose.
Broker is a required infrastructure dependency.
A team that skips the Broker and shares pact files via a shared folder loses versioning, can-i-deploy, and the verification audit trail.
The Pact workflow has four stages that run in two separate CI pipelines. The consumer pipeline (1 and 2) and the provider pipeline (3 and 4) are decoupled — they do not need to run simultaneously.
, the consumer writes a Pact consumer test. The test uses the

library to start a local mock HTTP server. The consumer code is pointed at that mock server rather than the real provider. The test sends a real request from the consumer code and asserts that the response was processed correctly. Pact records every interaction — the request that was sent and the response the mock returned — into a
, the pact file is published to a Pact Broker along with the consumer's version (typically the git SHA). The Broker stores the file and marks this combination as 'unverified'.

, the provider's CI job fetches all pact files for this provider from the Broker. It starts the real provider service (or a test double that runs the real business logic) and replays each interaction: it sends the exact request recorded in the pact file and checks that the real response satisfies every matcher the consumer specified.
, the provider verification result is published back to the Broker. The CI pipeline for either side can then call
to confirm that the version about to be deployed is compatible with every version of every other service currently in production.

attrs.alt: Pact contract testing flow for GenAI services

content.text: The diagram shows the four-stage flow. The
(an LLM orchestrator or tool-calling service) runs its Pact tests against a local mock server, which writes interactions to a
. The file is published to the
, which stores versioned pacts and verification results. The

(an LLM proxy, RAG service, or tool API) fetches the pact and runs its verification tests, posting results back to the Broker. The CI/CD gate queries the Broker's can-i-deploy endpoint before any deployment is allowed through.

Pact contract testing is most effective when two or more teams own opposite sides of an HTTP boundary and deploy independently. In GenAI systems, the characteristic scenarios are an orchestration team that calls a separately maintained LLM gateway, a retrieval team that owns a vector-search API called by the agent, and a platform team that owns shared tool microservices consumed by multiple agents.
two services are owned by different teams who want to deploy independently without coordinating a shared integration environment.
the provider API surface is under active development and changes frequently — contract tests give the provider team immediate feedback before a breaking change reaches consumers.
the service boundary carries an HTTP JSON API with well-defined request and response structures (tool call inputs/outputs, embedding request/response, etc.).
the consumer and provider are owned by the same team and deployed together — the overhead of the Broker is not justified and a shared integration test is simpler.

the provider is an external third-party API such as the Anthropic API or OpenAI API. You cannot run provider verification against services you do not control. Use Pact for the internal proxy or adapter you build around those external APIs.
. This ships the Pact Ruby core as a binary alongside the Python bindings and provides both
object. Use its context manager to start the mock server, define interactions with
, then run your real consumer code against the mock's base URL.
for fields where only the type matters. Use
for fields with a known pattern such as ISO dates. Use exact values only for fields whose exact content the consumer logic branches on.
Publish pact files to a Pact Broker. Run
as a CI step after the consumer tests pass.
pointing at the real provider base URL and the Broker. Call
. The Verifier fetches all registered pacts for this provider, replays each interaction, and reports which ones passed or failed.

Write provider state handlers if the provider is stateful. These are small Python functions that set up the database or in-memory state required for a specific interaction before the Verifier replays it.
Add can-i-deploy gates to both pipelines:
. The command exits non-zero if any required verification is missing or failed.
The complete workflow means both consumer and provider CI pipelines need access to the Pact Broker URL. Store the broker base URL and, if using PactFlow, the API token, as CI environment variables.
Consumer Test: LLM Proxy API Contract

# consumer/tests/test_llm_proxy_pact.py
# Defines what the orchestrator needs from the LLM proxy service.
# pip install pact-python pytest

import pytest
import requests
from pact import Consumer, Provider, Like, Term

PACT_DIR = "./pacts"
MOCK_HOST = "localhost"
MOCK_PORT = 1234

@pytest.fixture(scope="module")
def pact():
    """Start the Pact mock server for the duration of the module."""
    consumer = Consumer("llm-orchestrator")
    provider = Provider("llm-proxy")
    p = consumer.has_pact_with(
        provider,
        host_name=MOCK_HOST,
        port=MOCK_PORT,
        pact_dir=PACT_DIR,
    )
    p.start_service()
    yield p
    p.stop_service()


def test_orchestrator_calls_llm_proxy_and_reads_content(pact):
    """
    The orchestrator sends a /chat/completions POST and reads
    response.choices[0].message.content. The contract specifies:
      - the request body shape it sends
      - the minimum response fields it reads
    The mock responds with a representative example that satisfies the matchers.
    """
    expected_request_body = {
        "model": "gpt-4o-mini",
        "messages": [
            {"role": "system", "content": "You are a helpful assistant."},
            {"role": "user",   "content": "Summarise the meeting notes."},
        ],
        "max_tokens": 512,
    }
    expected_response = {
        "id": Like("chatcmpl-abc123"),
        "choices": [
            {
                "index": 0,
                "message": {
                    "role": "assistant",
                    "content": Like("Here is a summary of the meeting notes."),
                },
                "finish_reason": Term(r"stop|length|content_filter", "stop"),
            }
        ],
        "usage": {
            "prompt_tokens":     Like(50),
            "completion_tokens": Like(80),
            "total_tokens":      Like(130),
        },
    }

    (
        pact
        .given("the LLM proxy is healthy")
        .upon_receiving("a chat completion request from the orchestrator")
        .with_request(
            method="POST",
            path="/v1/chat/completions",
            headers={"Content-Type": "application/json"},
            body=expected_request_body,
        )
        .will_respond_with(200, body=expected_response)
    )

    # Run the real orchestrator code against the mock server.
    base_url = f"http://{MOCK_HOST}:{MOCK_PORT}"
    with pact:
        # This is the actual production function — not a stub.
        response = requests.post(
            f"{base_url}/v1/chat/completions",
            json=expected_request_body,
            headers={"Content-Type": "application/json"},
        )
        assert response.status_code == 200
        data = response.json()
        # The orchestrator code reads .choices[0].message.content
        content = data["choices"][0]["message"]["content"]
        assert isinstance(content, str)
        assert len(content) > 0
matcher in the response tells Pact: "the consumer only cares that this field exists and is the same type as the example, not that it has this exact value".

tells Pact to check the value against a regex pattern. These two matchers are the workhorses of consumer-driven contracts: they make the pact robust to cosmetic provider changes while still catching structural breaks.

# consumer/tests/test_search_tool_pact.py
# The orchestrator calls an internal search-tool microservice.
# Contract: POST /tools/search returns a list of result objects.

import json
import pytest
import requests
from pact import Consumer, Provider, Like, EachLike

@pytest.fixture(scope="module")
def pact():
    p = Consumer("llm-orchestrator").has_pact_with(
        Provider("search-tool"),
        host_name="localhost",
        port=1235,
        pact_dir="./pacts",
    )
    p.start_service()
    yield p
    p.stop_service()


def test_orchestrator_calls_search_tool_and_reads_results(pact):
    """
    The orchestrator sends a query and expects a list of results,
    each with 'title', 'url', and 'snippet' string fields.
    It does not care about the exact values — only the shape.
    """
    (
        pact
        .given("the search index has results for 'vector databases'")
        .upon_receiving("a search request from the orchestrator")
        .with_request(
            method="POST",
            path="/tools/search",
            headers={"Content-Type": "application/json"},
            body={"query": "vector databases", "max_results": 5},
        )
        .will_respond_with(
            200,
            headers={"Content-Type": "application/json"},
            body={
                "results": EachLike(
                    {
                        "title":   Like("Introduction to Vector Databases"),
                        "url":     Like("https://example.com/vector-db"),
                        "snippet": Like("Vector databases store and retrieve high-dimensional embeddings."),
                    },
                    minimum=1,
                )
            },
        )
    )

    with pact:
        resp = requests.post(
            "http://localhost:1235/tools/search",
            json={"query": "vector databases", "max_results": 5},
            headers={"Content-Type": "application/json"},
        )
        assert resp.status_code == 200
        results = resp.json()["results"]
        assert len(results) >= 1
        # The orchestrator reads these three fields from every result.
        for r in results:
            assert "title" in r
            assert "url" in r
            assert "snippet" in r

# provider/tests/test_llm_proxy_provider_verification.py
# Run on the LLM proxy team's CI to verify all registered consumer pacts.
# Requires the proxy server to be running locally on port 8080.

import pytest
from pact import Verifier

PROVIDER_BASE_URL = "http://localhost:8080"
BROKER_URL = "http://pact-broker:9292"  # or PactFlow URL
PROVIDER_NAME = "llm-proxy"
PROVIDER_VERSION = "abc1234"  # typically: os.environ["GIT_SHA"]


def test_llm_proxy_honours_consumer_pacts():
    """
    Fetches all pacts for 'llm-proxy' from the broker and
    replays each interaction against the running proxy server.
    Fails if any response does not match the matchers in the pact.
    """
    verifier = Verifier(
        provider=PROVIDER_NAME,
        provider_base_url=PROVIDER_BASE_URL,
    )

    # Provider state handlers: dict mapping state description -> callable.
    # The callable sets up whatever the service needs before replaying that interaction.
    provider_states = {
        "the LLM proxy is healthy": _ensure_proxy_healthy,
    }

    output, _ = verifier.verify_with_broker(
        broker_url=BROKER_URL,
        # Fetch pacts for the consumer versions currently deployed to production.
        enable_pending=True,
        publish_verification_results=True,
        provider_version=PROVIDER_VERSION,
        provider_states_setup_url=f"{PROVIDER_BASE_URL}/_pact/provider-states",
    )

    assert output == 0, "One or more Pact interactions failed verification"


def _ensure_proxy_healthy():
    """Provider state handler: verify the service is accepting requests."""
    import requests
    resp = requests.get(f"{PROVIDER_BASE_URL}/health")
    assert resp.status_code == 200
The provider verification step runs inside the
pipeline. It requires the real provider service to be running — either started as a subprocess in a
or already available in the CI environment. The key difference from the consumer side is that no mocks are involved: Pact sends real HTTP requests and evaluates real HTTP responses.

# .github/workflows/consumer-pact.yml
# Runs on the orchestrator (consumer) repository.

name: Consumer Pact Tests

on:
  push:
    branches: [main, "feature/**"]

env:
  PACT_BROKER_BASE_URL: ${{ secrets.PACT_BROKER_BASE_URL }}
  PACT_BROKER_TOKEN:    ${{ secrets.PACT_BROKER_TOKEN }}

jobs:
  consumer-pact:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Set up Python
        uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: "3.12"

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: pip install pact-python pytest requests

      - name: Run consumer Pact tests
        run: pytest consumer/tests/ -v
        # Tests write pact files to ./pacts/

      - name: Publish pacts to broker
        run: |
          pip install pact-python  # includes pact-broker CLI
          pact-broker publish ./pacts \
            --consumer-app-version ${{ github.sha }} \
            --branch ${{ github.ref_name }} \
            --broker-base-url $PACT_BROKER_BASE_URL \
            --broker-token $PACT_BROKER_TOKEN

      - name: Can I deploy? (consumer)
        run: |
          pact-broker can-i-deploy \
            --pacticipant llm-orchestrator \
            --version ${{ github.sha }} \
            --to-environment production \
            --broker-base-url $PACT_BROKER_BASE_URL \
            --broker-token $PACT_BROKER_TOKEN
Consider a production agentic platform with three services: an
that assembles tool calls and routes to an LLM, a
that wraps a vector database, and an
that normalises multiple model provider APIs into a single OpenAI-compatible endpoint. Three teams own these services. Each deploys independently several times a week.
The platform team introduced Pact after a search-tool release renamed
in the results array, breaking the orchestrator silently — the orchestrator had been reading
and falling back to an empty string on
, producing hallucinated summaries from the LLM for two hours before anyone noticed. The bug was invisible to both teams' unit tests.
After introducing Pact, the consumer test for the search tool explicitly named
matcher. When the search-tool team next ran their provider verification, the
field was absent from the actual response and the verification step failed immediately. The field rename was caught before merging, the search-tool team restored the
alias, and the two-hour outage pattern was eliminated.

In the same platform, the LLM proxy team uses provider states to test that the proxy correctly propagates model errors. A consumer test defines an interaction for the state "the upstream model returns a 429 rate-limit error" and expects the proxy to return a
with a specific JSON error body. The provider state handler for this interaction replaces the proxy's upstream client with a stub that returns a
. This contract-tested error path is something that was never covered by the team's previous integration tests, which only ran against a healthy model.
A consumer test that uses exact string values instead of
matchers will fail provider verification any time the provider generates a different value for that field — for example, if
is a random UUID. The fix is to use
for any field whose exact value the consumer does not branch on.
Using Pact to Replace Integration Tests Entirely

Pact verifies the contract at the wire boundary. It does not verify that the LLM proxy actually calls the upstream model correctly, that the search tool executes the right vector query, or that the orchestrator produces a coherent answer. A small set of end-to-end integration tests remains necessary to validate the system's behaviour as a whole. Pact should replace the bulk of integration tests for contract correctness, not all of them.

clause in the consumer test and a matching state handler in the provider, the provider verification replays the interaction against whatever state the service is in after startup. For stateful services this causes intermittent failures: the test passes when the database happens to have the right data and fails when it does not. Every interaction that depends on specific data must have a
Writing Pacts that Never Run in CI

Teams sometimes write consumer Pact tests but never wire them into the CI pipeline that publishes to the Broker. The pact files sit on developers' machines and are never verified by the provider. The full value of Pact comes only when
both sides run automatically in CI
and results are recorded in the Broker. Without publication and verification, the contracts are documentation-only with no enforcement.
string is a unique identifier for the interaction. Use a description that includes the consumer's intent and the scenario:
"a chat completion request from the orchestrator when the model is available"
for fields the consumer does not inspect by exact value. Use exact values for enum fields the consumer switches on (such as
Version pacts by git SHA, not by a mutable label.
cause the Broker to overwrite a previous result. Every consumer version that goes through CI should publish with

is set on the Verifier, pacts that have never been verified before are flagged as pending and do not fail the provider CI. This allows the consumer team to publish a new pact before the provider team has written the verification, without blocking the provider's pipeline.

Define consumer interactions for the failure modes the consumer handles: 429 rate-limit responses, 500 internal server errors, empty results arrays. These are the interactions most likely to expose missing error handling in the provider.
Wire the can-i-deploy check as a required CI step before any deployment to production, staging, or any shared environment. This is the safety net that makes independent deployment safe.
Consumer Pact tests run without any network calls to the actual provider because the Pact mock server is a local HTTP server started in-process by the

library. A suite of twenty consumer interactions typically completes in two to five seconds on a standard CI runner. The Pact mock server starts in under one second and stops cleanly when the context manager exits.
Consumer side scales with test count, not with real API latency.
There is no LLM call latency, no quota, and no network dependency. Adding more interactions costs only the overhead of a local HTTP round-trip.
Provider verification cost depends on provider startup time.

If the provider is a FastAPI service it starts in under two seconds; if it requires database migrations it may take longer. Isolate provider tests from integration tests to keep the suite fast — start the provider once per test session, not once per test.
The Pact Broker is not in the hot path of production traffic.
It is only queried during CI and at deploy time. A self-hosted Broker on a small VM or in a Docker container handles hundreds of pacts and verifications without performance constraints.
If a provider has contracts with many consumers, the Verifier can run consumer pacts in parallel. PactFlow's managed Broker also supports provider verification result streaming for faster feedback.
Python consumer and provider Pact library
Wraps the Pact Ruby core; supports HTTP and message pacts; pip install pact-python
Uses the pact-reference Rust core; supports Pact Specification v4; actively developed as the v1 replacement
Docker image pactfoundation/pact-broker; stores pacts, verification results, and environment records; includes a web UI and can-i-deploy API
Hosted by SmartBear; adds bi-directional contract testing, team dashboards, SAML SSO, and audit logs; free tier for open-source projects
Mature library for Java Spring Boot providers; compatible pact files work across pact-python and pact-jvm in the same Broker
Useful when the consumer is a Next.js frontend or a Node.js BFF that calls GenAI tool APIs
Included in pact-python installation; used for publish, can-i-deploy, record-deployment, and environment management commands
Yes — all services must run simultaneously
Full system smoke tests before release
Baseline shape enforcement without a Broker
UI regression; catches unintended changes but over-specifies
Providers with existing OpenAPI specs; no provider verification harness needed
Pact sits between schema validation (which is provider-driven and has no deploy gate) and full integration testing (which requires live services). Its unique value is the
direction — the people who actually call an API define what they need from it — combined with the automated

safety gate. Bi-directional contract testing (BDCT in PactFlow) is a useful alternative when the provider already publishes an OpenAPI spec and writing a provider verification harness would be prohibitively expensive.

Pact files are stored in the Pact Broker and may contain sensitive request shapes — including API key header names, prompt templates, and the exact JSON structure of tool inputs. Treat the Broker as a production internal service. The self-hosted Pact Broker supports basic authentication and can be placed behind a reverse proxy that enforces mTLS. PactFlow uses API tokens with role-based access control.
Do not include real credentials in pact files.
Consumer tests should use placeholder values for Authorization headers:
, not a real API key. Pact only checks that the header is present and the value matches the pattern, not that the token authenticates with the real service.
environment variables enable HTTP basic auth. Store the Broker credentials in CI secrets, never in source code.
Make can-i-deploy a required status check.
Configure the can-i-deploy result as a required GitHub Actions check on protected branches. A deployment that bypasses this check can break a dependent service in production with no early warning.
. This tells the Broker which versions are live in which environment, making can-i-deploy checks accurate.
Limit provider state handlers' blast radius.

Provider state handlers run during verification and modify service state. Use a dedicated test database for verification runs, isolated from the development or staging database. A poorly written state handler that truncates a shared table can corrupt team-shared test data.

Pact is a consumer-driven contract testing framework: the consumer writes tests that specify what it needs from each provider, those expectations are serialised to a pact file, and the provider verifies them independently — no live service-to-service communication required during testing.

For GenAI architectures, Pact protects the boundaries between an LLM orchestrator and the tool APIs, LLM proxies, and retrieval services it calls — catching field renames, missing fields, and changed status codes before they cause silent failures in production.
matchers for fields whose exact value the consumer does not branch on; use exact values only for enum or discriminator fields that drive consumer logic.
The Pact Broker is the coordination layer: it stores versioned pacts and verification results, and its
endpoint is what makes independent deployment of multiple GenAI services safe.

Pact does not verify model quality or business logic — only the wire API contract. Combine it with evals testing, integration tests for critical end-to-end flows, and unit tests for in-process logic to achieve full coverage.

Provider state handlers are required for stateful providers — write them for every interaction that depends on specific data, and run verification against an isolated test database to prevent state leakage.
Bi-directional contract testing (PactFlow BDCT) is a pragmatic alternative when the provider already maintains an OpenAPI spec, eliminating the need to write a provider verification harness.
